query: japan carry trade unwind august 2024 crash

videos.title: Japan's Yen Carry Trade: The Hidden Bet That Could Crash Markets
videos.text: This is Milo. One ordinary morning last August, he opened his investing app and a chunk of his savings had simply vanished overnight. He hadn't sold anything. He hadn't done anything. A central bank on the other side of the world had nudged a single interest rate and a trade most people have never even heard of came violently apart, >> [music] >> dragging markets down with it. In one day, Japan's stock market fell more than 12% its worst day since 1987. And the shockwave hit New York, London, everywhere. The culprit had a boring name. The Yen carry trade. [music] It might be the most important trade you've never heard of. A hidden wire running underneath the entire financial system. So, let's pull on it. Here's the whole idea. For 25 years, Japan kept its interest rates pinned near zero. Money was almost free. So, traders around the world started doing this. Borrow Yen for next to nothing, convert it into dollars, and use that to buy things that actually pay a yield. US government bonds, stocks, even crypto. Then, pocket the difference. Borrow at half a percent in Tokyo, earn 5% in New York, and you keep the 4 and 1/2% gap for doing essentially nothing. Now, pile on borrowed leverage and that thin sliver becomes a fortune. That same leverage is exactly what makes it dangerous. But, we'll get to that. How big is this trade? Honestly, nobody knows precisely. Estimates run from a few hundred billion dollars to several trillion, depending on what you count. The famous 4 trillion figure is really just a guess no one can pin down. But everyone agrees on this. It's enormous. Big enough to move the whole world. And it isn't just Wall Street hedge funds. It's global banks. It's pension funds. And famously, ordinary Japanese households. Picture a retiree we'll call Mrs. Tanaka quietly shifting her savings into foreign assets for a better return. Millions of small bets and a few giant ones all riding the same trade. That's the pipe quietly funding markets everywhere. So what changed? After decades stuck at zero, the Bank of Japan finally started raising rates. And in June 2026, it lifted its rate to 1%. That sounds tiny. But for Japan, it's the first real hiking cycle in about 31 years. And yet the yen kept falling. All the way to around 161 to the dollar. It's weakest level since 1986. A near 40-year low. Why is it still sinking? Because even at 1%, Japan's rate is still far below America's near 3.75. [music] That gap is the entire engine of the carry trade. So as long as it stays this wide, the pressure just keeps building. Japan is fighting it. In just 2 months this spring, Tokyo spent a record 11.7 trillion yen, about 73 billion dollars, propping up its own currency. Now it's finance minister and the US Treasury secretary are openly coordinating hinting at decisive action. When governments start talking like that, markets get nervous. And they should be. Because this is not hypothetical. It already happened in miniature just last August. The Bank of Japan nudged rates up. A weak US jobs report landed two days later, and the yen suddenly jumped. That was all it took. In a single Monday, the unwind tore through the markets. Japan's Nikkei fell 12.4% its worst day since the crash of 1987. The markets fear gauge, the VIX, spiked to 65, a level seen only in the 2008 crisis and the COVID crash. America's S&P dropped 3% in sympathy. Then, almost as fast, it bounced. The Nikkei posted one of its biggest gains ever the next day. Once a Bank of Japan official promised they wouldn't hike into the chaos. A near miss. But it showed everyone exactly how the fuse works. Which brings [music] us to the real question. How does a small rate move in Tokyo wipe out savings in New York? Through a chain reaction. And it's worth watching step by step. It starts when the yen jumps. Suddenly everyone who borrowed yen owes more than before. Their debts are underwater. Their lenders demand more collateral. That's a margin call. So, they're forced to sell, fast. And what do they sell? The very things the carry trade bought. US stocks, bonds, emerging market assets. At the same time they buy yen back to repay the loans, which pushes the yen up even further, which puts even more traders underwater. Round and round it goes. That's the part to remember. Leverage is the hidden wiring that connects a currency in Tokyo [music] to the retirement account of someone who has never once thought about Japan. When it unwinds, everything sells at once, which is why a Japanese interest rate can ruin a Monday in Manhattan. So, why doesn't Japan just fix this? Because it's trapped between two walls. Hike rates too fast and it triggers exactly this unwind, plus a recession at home. Hike too slow and the yen keeps collapsing, making everything Japan imports, energy, food, more expensive for its own people. And there's a deeper reason it can't simply raise rates. Debt. Japan's government owes about 235% of its entire economy, the highest in the developed world. Already, a quarter of the national budget goes just to paying interest. Higher rates would blow a hole in its own finances. Japan, quite literally, cannot afford normal interest rates. Now, is this a guaranteed catastrophe? Not necessarily, and honest analysts push back. Much of the riskiest carry was already flushed out back in 2024. Some argue it's a funding shift, not a credit crisis, and that even a large 20% [music] unwind, around $400 billion, is small against a $70 trillion US stock market. The fuse is real. Whether it actually blows is genuinely debated. But, here's why the whole world watches anyway. Japan is one of the planet's biggest creditors and the single largest foreign owner of US government debt, about 1.24 trillion dollars of it. When the country that quietly funds everyone else starts pulling its money home, everyone feels the tug. So, what do you actually watch? Three things. The yen, around 160 to the dollar, the line where Tokyo tends to step in. The Bank of Japan's next meetings, and that gap between US and Japanese rates. When those move, the carry trade moves, and [music] so can your portfolio. Because that's the real lesson of the great unwind. Some of the biggest risks in finance aren't loud or obvious. They're quiet machines humming away under everything that almost no one notices. Until the day they stop. Japan's carry trade has powered global markets for a generation, and nobody is quite sure how it ends. This has been Capital World Order, making the hidden machinery of money make sense. None of this is financial advice. If it helped, subscribe and tell me what to explain next. Thanks for watching.

videos.text: $33 billion in 30 days. That is more than the entire GDP of most countries spent in one single month just to stop a collapse, not a war, not a natural disaster, not a recession. Not the largest this year, not the largest this decade. The largest single intervention Japan has ever conducted. Period. For context, Japan's previous monthly record was 62 billion. That matches almost exactly the size of the intervention operation. The Federal Reserve's own custody data confirmed foreign Treasury selling occurred. Japan liquidated American assets to defend its own collapsing currency simultaneously. So, while spending $73 billion to buy yen, Japan simultaneously was dumping American bonds onto global markets at the same time. Two financial weapons fired at once, both hitting US markets. And here is why this matters directly to every American watching. When Japan dumps US Treasuries, Treasury yields rise in America. When Treasury yields rise, mortgage rates climb across the entire country. The 30-year US mortgage rate is already pressing towards 6.22%. Every American trying to buy a home is feeling Japan's crisis. Every American carrying a variable rate loan is feeling Japan's crisis. Every American with a 401k loaded with bonds is exposed now. Part two reveals the most alarming data point in this entire story. Finance Vision has the numbers. Stay right there for part two. Part one established the twin decisions that shook global markets simultaneously. BOJ up 1%, highest since 1995. Fed held but signaled hikes ahead. Dow fell 507 points. Two-year yields jumped 16 basis points. Gold fell. Now the mortgage rate dimension, the most direct American household consequence. Vishal Garg, CEO of AI mortgage platform Better, said, "Right now, this is about what the Fed says, not just what it does. Mortgage rates track long-term Treasury yields, which move based on investor expectations for inflation, growth, and the Fed's next step." Investing.com. Mortgage rates track Treasury yields. Treasury yields track Fed expectations. Fed signaled hikes. Chen Zhao, head of economics research at Redfin, said, "It's clear that we're in a new era, and it's going to take a while for markets to figure out how to react to today's Fed meeting. One thing is certain, the committee as a whole is taking inflation very seriously, which means mortgage rates are unlikely to retreat much in the near future." Investing.com. Unlikely to retreat. Redfin's head of economics research said that not a contrarian view. Mortgage rates briefly dropped into the high fives in January after Trump directed Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy $200 billion in mortgage bonds before rebounding into the low sixes and ultimately soaring again after the Iran war broke out. Science Direct soaring again after the Iran war. Rates that briefly improved are now climbing again. And the June 17th Fed statement just confirmed that the next move is more likely up. Traders are now pricing in a 49% chance of a rate hike in September, up from a 27% chance the day before the meeting. Markets were holding out hope Warsh would deliver some dovishness that they obviously felt they did not get. Investing.com, 49% September hike probability, doubled from 27% in 24 hours. One press conference did that. That is the speed at which Warsh's communication style is reshaping market expectations. Now the carry trade dimension and the AEI warning that every American must read. The challenge to world financial markets is that the narrowing of the interest rate spread between Japanese bonds and US bonds could lead to an unwinding of the Japanese carry trade and the repatriation of capital to Japan. A process that could be reinforced by a meaningful appreciation of the Japanese yen, currently estimated to be undervalued by 15 to 20%. Zoya. Undervalued 15-20% even after the June 16th hike. The yen has room to strengthen materially. The prospective comes at an awkward time for the United States. It comes at the very time when Trump's large tax cuts have increased the US government's budget deficit to an annual rate of around $2 trillion. The last thing the US government needs is the selling of its bonds by one of its major creditors. Zoya. Two trillion deficit. Japan, its largest foreign creditor. Carry trade unwinding simultaneously. That could contribute to a rise in US government bond yields. That could be a trigger for the bursting of the artificial intelligence bubble. We should brace ourselves for some rough financial market sledding in the year ahead on the back of a meaningful rise in US Treasury bond yields. Zoya AI bubble. That is the AEI connecting Japan's rate hike to Silicon Valley's valuation premium. Not a peripheral risk. The AI bubble is built on low discount rates. Higher yields compress it. Rehook, BOJ at 1%, Fed signaling hike, AEI warning AI bubble, mortgage rates soaring. Now the bond purchase taper, the second tool the BOJ fired simultaneously with the hike. The central bank said it will continue reducing its government bond purchases by 200 billion yen per calendar quarter before halting the taper and maintaining monthly JGB purchases of 2 trillion yen from April 2027. The Canadian Vanguard from Y 8.5 5.7 trillion per month in August 2024 to Y 2 trillion by April 2027. That is a 65% reduction in BOJ bond market support in under 3 years. When the BOJ buys fewer JGBs, domestic institutional investors must source those bonds elsewhere. Pension funds and life insurers that buy BOJ vacated JGB supply have less capital for US Treasuries. Less capital for US Treasuries means fewer buyers at American Treasury auctions. Fewer buyers means higher yields demanded. Higher yields means higher American mortgage rates. The QT is not separate from the rate hike. Both tools pressure American bond demand simultaneously. Yields on 10-year Japanese government bonds climbed three basis points to 2.615% immediately after the BOJ decision. And the yen strengthened marginally to 160.022 against the dollar remaining near the intervention threshold. The Canadian Vanguard 2.615% on Japan's 10-year versus hedged US Treasury return of approximately 1.3%. That 130 basis point domestic advantage pulls Japanese institutional capital home every single day. Now, the Warsh factor. The structural change in how America's central bank communicates. The FOMC's post-meeting statement removed prior language seen as a nod toward an easing slant. The statement was unanimously approved with a notably shorter, crisper format that Warsh described as giving you the facts as best we can judge them. Bloomberg easing language eliminated. Forward guidance eliminated. A fundamentally different Fed era begins. Warsh confirmed he had withheld his own dot submission. It's not helpful in the conduct of policy. And is forming five task forces to review communication, data, inflation metrics, productivity, and labor markets by year end 2026. Science direct no warsh. No forward guidance. Five task forces reforming the Fed from the inside. Markets cannot model a Fed that will not tell them where rates are going next quarter. And that uncertainty premium embedded in treasury yields is itself inflationary for borrowing costs. Six FOMC members project two 25 basis point hikes before year end. PCE inflation at 3.6% year end, highest projection in 3 years. GDP growth slowing to 2.2% down from 2.4% in March projections. The Edge Malaysia. Inflation rising, growth slowing. Six members projecting two hikes. That is the stagflation signal. Not declared stagflation, but the trajectory of rising prices alongside slowing growth is visible. Now, three specific actions for every American watching Finance Unlocked right now. First, understand your mortgage rate context directly from June 17th data. The 10-year US Treasury sits at 4.43% after the Fed meeting sent yields higher. September hike probability just doubled to 49%. If that lands, yields go higher still. Every American with a variable rate mortgage faces the next payment adjustment with this backdrop. Every American shopping for a home faces the same 4.43% Treasury as their rate reference point. Second, watch Japanese institutional repatriation data in the next TIC release. Japan spent 73.5 billion dollars on yen intervention in May, funded by US Treasury sales. The BOJ hiked to 1%, but the yen is still near 160. The carry trade still persists. As long as the yen stays near 160, Japan may need to intervene again, selling more bonds. With the yen still weak near 160, the risk of sudden Japanese intervention sits over every short yen position in the market. The 7-to-1 vote and the slower balance sheet run off signal Japan is still normalizing, but the easy consensus is fraying. Federal Reserve still fraying. Still near 160, still potentially intervening by selling American debt next week. Third, the AI exposure question. The AEI's warning is not theoretical. It is mathematical. Higher discount rates from rising Treasury yields compress every growth stocks valuation directly. The Nasdaq fell 1.34% from one Fed press conference. That is the sensitivity we are tracking. If Japan's carry trade continues unwinding as BOJ hikes toward 1.75% terminal, the math compounds. Every 25 basis points added to long-term US yields from Japanese repatriation or Fed hikes means every high multiple technology stocks intrinsic valuation falls by the same mathematical discount rate formula that underpins every equity pricing model on Wall Street. The BOJ's June 16th decision is not an isolated event. The Fed's June 17th decision is not an isolated event. Together, with the Dow down 507 points, two-year yields at 4.21% and mortgage rates refusing to fall, they represent the clearest signal that the era of cheap global money that powered the American asset price rally from 2010 to 2025 is now structurally and permanently over. That is the complete breakdown. Share this immediately with every investor in your network who thinks June 17th was just another Fed meeting. It was not. Subscribe to Finance Unlocked for the global financial intelligence that connects Tokyo's central bank to your mortgage, your retirement, and your financial future in real time. Drop a comment. Do you think the Fed hikes in September and how are you positioning your portfolio? We read every single one. See you in the next one.

videos.title: When Japan's Carry Trade Explodes
videos.text: Something dangerous is happening in Japan. While the world was focused on Davos, Japan's bond market quietly crossed a threshold it hadn't touched in 30 years. 40-year Japanese government bond yields broke 4%. For the first time since the 1990s, when bond yields rise, bond prices fall. Investors are selling fast. They're asking, "Will Japan's economy even exist in 40 years?" And they're not waiting around to find out. For three decades, Japan was the land of no inflation. Prices stayed flat. Interest rates stayed at zero. But now inflation has surged to 3%. Investors are doing the math. If inflation is 3% and bond yields are lower than that, they lose money just by holding the bonds. So they're demanding higher returns, at least 4%. And yields are spiking. The Bank of Japan is scrambling. They raised their benchmark rate to 0.75%, the highest level since 1995. By mid 2026, it's expected to hit 1%. But here's the problem. Japan is the world's most indebted nation. Debt to GDP ratio, 260%. The highest on the planet. When interest rates rise, Japan has to pay more interest on that massive debt. Growth gets eaten alive. But if they keep rates low, inflation runs wild and the yen becomes worthless. It's a trap. And it gets worse. The Bank of Japan used to buy 50% of all Japanese government bonds. But now they're tightening. They're buying less. Foreign investors. They're fleeing. Foreign ownership of Japanese bonds is at its lowest level since 2019. The second largest bond market in the world is freezing up. Liquidity is vanishing. But the real danger isn't the bond market. It's what happens when the yen carry trade unwinds. For 30 years, Japan's interest rates were zero. Global investors treated Japan like a free ATM. Borrow yen at 0%. Invest in US stocks, high yield assets, anything with returns. Pocket the difference. It was the greatest trade in modern finance. Trillions of dollars flow through this system. But now the conditions are reversing. Japan is raising rates. The US might cut rates. The interest rate gap between Japan and the US is shrinking. And if global instability drives money into safe haven yen, the currency could surge. When that happens, the yen carry trade collapses. Here's how it works. You borrowed yen when $1 equals 150 yen. But now you have to repay when $1 equals 130 yen. Suddenly, your debt is worth far more. You're sitting on massive currency losses and you're drowning in debt. Banks and brokers issue margin calls. Add more collateral or we liquidate your positions. Now, this is stage one, the margin call. Investors are forced to sell their US assets to repay their yen loans. Stage two, liquidation. In August 2024, Japan raised rates just slightly. Bitcoin crashed. US stocks tanked. That was a warning shot. If yen carry unwinding becomes full scale, we're looking at a global capital exodus. The biggest targets, US mega cap tech stocks, the Magnificent 7, the NASDAQ, US Treasury bonds, the most liquid assets in the world. It doesn't matter how strong a company's fundamentals are. When a liquidity shock this large hits, everything gets sold to raise cash. Indiscriminate liquidation. Blue chip stocks collapse alongside everything else. Japan is trapped in an impossible trilmma. They need to one suppress interest rates, two defend the currency, three maintain fiscal stability. They can't do all three. If Japan defends the yen by selling its US Treasury holdings, US interest rates spike. America has strong incentives to prevent that. So, the Federal Reserve is secretly lending Japan dollars through currency swap lines to help defend the yen. Recently, when the yen hit 160 per dollar, it suddenly reversed. That wasn't market forces. That was intervention. But this is a painkiller, not a cure. Eventually, Japan will have to sacrifice its currency. Accept inflation. Let the yen weaken. When Japan, the free ATM of global finance, disappears, markets will convulse. We've seen this before. 2008, Lehman Brothers collapsed. Trust in the financial system evaporated. Investors went into extreme riskoff mode. They rushed to buy back yen to close their carry trades. The yen surged. Global stock markets collapsed. Emerging market currencies imploded. Commodities and real estate cratered. The yen carry unwind was the amplifier of the crisis. 2020 CO 19 initial shock. Panic spread faster than the virus. Investors scrambled for cash. The yen surged again as carry trades unwound. The S&P 500 plunged. Bitcoin wasn't spared either. It dropped 37% in a single day. December 2025, the Bank of Japan raised rates to 0.75%. the highest level in 30 years. Japan is abandoning its ultra- low rate policy. Cryptocurrency markets are already shaking. Gold and silver safe haven assets are surging. Crypto is falling before stocks. Why? Liquidity and leverage. When riskoff mode hits, investors sell the most liquid assets first. Bitcoin trades 24/7. Settlement is instant. It's the easiest asset to liquidate. And crypto is heavily leveraged through derivatives. When forced liquidations begin, they cascade. One margin call triggers another, then another. A liquidation contagion. Stock markets are still holding steady. But if the Bank of Japan raises rates again, equities will shake, too. Yen carry liquidation isn't about fundamentals. It's not about company earnings or valuations. It's about global liquidity choking. It's a macro phenomenon driven by forced selling and supply demand distortions. The Bank of Japan's next rate decision could trigger the avalanche. That's why the world is watching Japan. Not because of its economy, because of the trillions of dollars borrowed against its currency. The yen bomb is ticking. And when it goes off, no asset class is safe. Not stocks, not crypto, not bonds. The free money era is ending. And the unwind will be brutal. If you want to see how money really works, not what they teach in textbooks, but the leverage, the margin calls, and the liquidity traps that move trillions in seconds, subscribe to Endgame Economics, because by the time this hits mainstream headlines, your portfolio will already be bleeding. Stay aware, stay prepared, stay ready.

videos.title: Japan's Yen Collapse! America's NEXT FINANCIAL CRISIS Has Begun.
videos.text: On June 30th, 2026, the Japanese yen fell to 162 per dollar. That is the weakest the yen has been against the dollar since 1986, a 40-year low. Now, you might be thinking, "That sounds like Japan's problem, a currency I have never held, in a country I may have never visited, with an economy I have probably never thought about." Here is why that is exactly wrong. The Japanese yen is not just Japan's currency. For the last 30 years, it has been the hidden engine underneath the American stock market, underneath US Treasury prices, underneath your mortgage rate, and underneath the retirement account you're counting on. And when that engine breaks, which the data is now saying it is very close to doing, the first casualty is not in Tokyo. It is in your brokerage account, your bond fund, your 401k. By the end of this video, you're going to understand exactly how the yen carry trade has been quietly subsidizing US asset prices for decades. You're going to understand what happens when that subsidy reverses, and you are going to understand the specific signal that tells you the reversal has begun. Because that signal is now flashing in the data in a way it is only flashed twice before. Both times, US markets fell sharply within weeks. Stay with me, because what I am going to show you, the specific number Michael Burry has been pointing at for the entire first half of 2026, and once you see it, you cannot unsee it. Let me start with the machine, because you have to understand what it does before you can understand what happens when it breaks. Imagine you could borrow money for almost nothing, 0.1% not from a credit card, not from a bank, from the entire Japanese financial system, unlimited for 30 years. Now, imagine you take that money and invest it in US Treasury bonds paying 4%. The profit is the difference, nearly four full percentage points on whatever you borrow. The more you borrow, the more you make. And you make it every single year as long as the gap stays open. That is the carry trade. And it is not a theory. It peaked at between 300 billion and 500 billion dollars in outstanding positions according to Bloomberg and Stapleton Asset Management. But here is the part most explanations stop at and the part you actually need. The hedge funds borrowing yen and buying US assets get all the attention. They are the cartoon version of the story. The real carry trade is much bigger, much deeper and it is coming from inside Japan itself. Japan's pension funds, life insurance companies and major financial institutions have accumulated decades of current account surpluses. Every year Japan sells more to the world than it buys. Those excess dollars have to go somewhere and for 30 years they have gone to the United States. Japanese investors used accumulated dollars to buy US equities and bonds mostly on an unhedged basis. They were willing to assume the currency risk because the yen had been weakening for years and the Bank of Japan kept policy rates at almost zero. So the trade is not just hedge funds borrowing cheap yen. It is the entire weight of Japan's institutional savings flowing continuously into US stocks, US bonds and US dollar assets because there was simply nothing worth buying at home. That flow has been one of the primary sources of demand for US Treasuries helping finance American deficits and keeping Treasury yields lower than they would otherwise be. It has been one of the primary sources of demand for US equities providing a continuous structural bid underneath the stock market. It has been in other words an invisible subsidy to every American who owns a retirement account and it is now in the process of breaking. Japan deployed approximately 72.5 billion dollars in currency interventions between late April and late May 2026 buying yen, selling dollars to stop the currency's fall. The yen kept falling anyway. The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark interest rate to 1% on June 16th the highest since 1995. The first time rates have been this high in 30 years. The yen kept falling anyway. It hit 162 per dollar on June 30th and here's what makes that genuinely alarming. 72.5 billion in intervention, rate hike to a 30-year high both in the same 2 months. And the yen hit a 40-year low regardless. That is not a currency under pressure, that is a currency being pushed by forces that are larger than the Japanese government's ability to contain them. The forces are the carry trade itself. Decades of capital flow decisions by Japanese pension funds, insurance companies, and institutional investors who have found consistently better risk-adjusted returns outside Japan than inside it. And the Japanese government has finally, after decades of treating the symptom with FX intervention, identified the real cause. Japan's finance minister just called for the nation's pension funds and institutional investors to repatriate and increase their investments in domestic Japanese assets. The implicit message, 900 billion dollars of US assets held by Japanese institutions should come home. I want to be honest about what is likely and what is not. The world's largest pension fund, Japan's government pension investment fund with 1.81 trillion in total assets, roughly half in US markets, reviews its allocation once every 5 years. The last review was in 2025. The next is not until 2030. It will not be forced to move in the next 6 months. But, here is what that honest caveat does not change. The direction of travel is now officially stated government policy. And the market knows what happened the last time a much smaller version of this trade started to unwind. August 5th, 2024, the Bank of Japan raised rates from 0.1% to 0.25%. That is 15 basis points, less than a quarter of a percentage point. The Nikkei fell 20% in 5 days, the worst fall since 1987. The Nasdaq fell over 10% in 2 weeks. US bond markets experienced significant volatility. Volatility exploded globally. All of that from a 15 basis point rate hike in Tokyo. Now, the Bank of Japan's rate is at 1%. Japan's 10-year government bond yield has climbed to approximately 2.8%. A level not seen since 1997. For the first time in a generation, Japanese pension funds and insurance companies can earn meaningful returns at home without taking on currency risk by parking money in US Treasuries. The incentive structure that drove the carry trade is reversing. Not all at once, not overnight, but in the same direction that it reversed in August 2024. At that time, a 15 basis point move was enough to shake the entire global financial system. Here's where I want to stop and show you the specific mechanism that connects the yen to your retirement account. Because this is the part most coverage of this story does not explain clearly enough. When Japanese institutional investors decide to repatriate capital, to bring money home from US assets, here is exactly what happens in sequence. They sell US stocks and US bonds. This increases supply of those assets without a corresponding increase in demand. Treasury yields rise as prices fall. Equity prices fall as selling pressure exceeds buying. They convert the dollars they receive back into yen. This increases demand for yen and increases supply of dollars. The yen strengthens, the dollar weakens. Here's the loop that makes this dangerous. As the yen strengthens, the carry trade becomes less profitable. The interest rate gap between Japan and the US is already narrower than it has been in 30 years. If the yen is also appreciating rather than depreciating, the currency loss on the trade starts to overwhelm the interest rate gain. More carry traders unwind, more yen is bought. The yen strengthens further, more carry traders unwind. This is the self-reinforcing mechanism that every serious analyst is watching. And it has a name, the carry trade unwind cascade. The August 2024 episode was a preview. A small BOJ rate hike triggered a cascade that wiped 20% off the Nikkei and 10% off the Nasdaq in 2 weeks before central banks coordinated to stabilize markets. That was the small version. The version that started from 0.1% and went to 0.25%. The current version starts from 72.5 billion in failed interventions, a 40-year currency low, 1% BOJ rates, 2.8% Japanese bond yields, and an official government policy of repatriation. The starting conditions are dramatically worse. Here's the direct question for you right now. When you think about your retirement account, your 401K, your IRA, your pension, do you think about it as being exposed to the Japanese yen? Most Americans would say no. But based on everything you now know about how Japanese institutional capital has been flowing continuously into US equities and bonds for decades, the honest answer is yes. Every American who holds US stocks or bonds in a retirement account has been, without knowing it, a beneficiary of the yen carry trade. Drop a comment below. Does this change how you think about your portfolio? Have you done anything to position for a yen reversal? I read every single comment. Your answer shapes the next video directly. Now, let me show you the shadow data, the verified numbers that are almost completely absent from mainstream coverage of this story. The first number, Japan's government debt exceeds 250% of GDP, the highest among all major developed economies on Earth by a significant margin. The United States is at 101% and analysts are calling it unsustainable. Japan is at 250%. This matters because it directly constrains how aggressively the Bank of Japan can raise rates to defend the yen.
